title: The Day of the Lord and the New Heavens
subtitle: Scoffers dismissed, divine patience explained, cosmic renewal promised

heb.paragraph: Scoffers (empaiktai) will come in the last days - those who ridicule the promise of Christ's return. Mockery is a sign of the end.
Following their own sinful desires - the mockers are not intellectually honest skeptics but morally motivated deniers.

heb.gloss: to bring together, hold together, constitute
heb.paragraph: By the word of God heavens existed and earth was formed (synestosa) - creation was by divine speech; so will be destruction.

refs.note: In the last time there will be scoffers, following their own ungodly passions.
God said, Let the waters be gathered.
The fountains of the great deep burst forth.
Of old you laid the foundation of the earth.

notes.note: This is my second letter to stir up your sincere mind, to remember prophetic words and the apostolic commandment.
Scoffers will come in the last days, mocking: Where is the promise of his coming? Everything continues as from creation.
They deliberately overlook that by God's word heavens and earth were formed from water, and the world perished by water. Present heavens and earth are stored for fire.

Calvin notes that uniformitarianism (all continues as always) is not a new argument. The flood refutes it; fire awaits. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: All things continue as they were from creation - this is philosophical uniformitarianism, denying divine intervention.
Deliberately overlook (lanthano) - the ignorance is willful, not accidental. They suppress inconvenient truth.
Stored up for fire - the destruction will be by fire, not water. The same powerful word that created will destroy.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes that the scoffers are not neutral skeptics but morally compromised. Their mockery serves their lifestyle. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

davids.source: Peter Davids, NICNT/Pillar 2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids notes that Peter uses the flood as precedent. If God intervened once, he can and will intervene again. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.historical: Second Peter addresses the threat of false teachers who deny Christ's return (parousia) and promote moral license. The author presents himself as the apostle Peter writing shortly before his death (1:14), though many scholars date the letter later due to its developed theology, reference to Paul's letters as 'Scripture' (3:16), and literary dependence on Jude. The false teachers mock the delay of Christ's return ('Where is the promise of his coming?' 3:4) and use this skepticism to justify immoral living. The letter defends apostolic eyewitness testimony (1:16-18), warns of judgment using Old Testament examples (2:4-10), and explains that God's patience allows time for repentance (3:9).
hist.context: Peter writes to stir up sincere minds, reminding them of prophetic words and apostolic commands. In the last days scoffers will come, mocking the promise of Christ's coming. They ask: Where is the promise? All continues as from creation. They deliberately overlook that by God's word heavens existed and earth was formed from water, and that the ancient world was destroyed by water. By the same word, present heavens and earth are stored up for fire, kept for judgment day and destruction of the ungodly.

heb.gloss: to be patient, slow to anger
heb.paragraph: The Lord is patient (makrothumei) toward you - delay is not inability but patience. He desires all to reach repentance.
The day of the Lord will come like a thief - unexpected, unannounced. Readiness is essential.
New heavens and a new earth (kainos) - not merely renewed but qualitatively different, where righteousness dwells.

refs.note: A thousand years in your sight are but as yesterday.
The day of the Lord will come like a thief in the night.
Behold, I create new heavens and a new earth.
I saw a new heaven and a new earth.

notes.note: With the Lord, one day is as a thousand years. The Lord is not slow but patient, not wishing any to perish but all to reach repentance.
The day of the Lord will come like a thief. Heavens will pass away with a roar; elements will be burned; earth and its works exposed.
Since all will be dissolved, what sort of people ought you to be? Waiting for and hastening the day. We wait for new heavens and new earth where righteousness dwells.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Not wishing any to perish - the desire is universal, though the accomplishment is particular. God's heart is for all.
Elements (stoicheia) may mean basic components or elemental spirits. Either way, cosmic dissolution is comprehensive.
Hastening (speudo) the day - believers may hasten the parousia through holy living and mission. Their conduct affects timing.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes eschatological ethics. The certainty of cosmic dissolution should produce holiness, not nihilism. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

davids.source: Peter Davids, NICNT/Pillar 2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids notes the tension between divine sovereignty (the day will come) and human responsibility (hastening). Both are affirmed. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Peter addresses the apparent delay. With the Lord, one day is as a thousand years and a thousand years as one day. The Lord is not slow as some count slowness but is patient, not wishing any to perish but all to reach repentance. The day of the Lord will come like a thief; heavens will pass away with a roar, elements will be burned, earth and its works exposed. Since all will be dissolved, what sort of people ought you to be in holiness and godliness, waiting and hastening the coming day? According to his promise, we wait for new heavens and new earth where righteousness dwells.

heb.paragraph: Be diligent (spoudasate) to be found by him without spot or blemish and at peace - effort is required for readiness.
Without spot or blemish (aspiloi kai amometoi) - the opposite of the false teachers who are spots and blemishes (2:13).
Grow in grace and knowledge - steadfastness prevents being carried away by error. Growth is the antidote to apostasy.

refs.note: Do you presume on the riches of his kindness and patience?
No longer be children, tossed by every wind of doctrine.
Walk in a manner worthy of the Lord, bearing fruit and growing in knowledge.
To him who is able to keep you from stumbling.

notes.note: Be diligent to be found without spot or blemish, at peace. Count the patience of our Lord as salvation.
As our beloved brother Paul wrote with wisdom given him. His letters have hard things that the ignorant twist to their destruction.
You know this beforehand, so guard yourselves. Do not be carried away by error. Grow in grace and knowledge. To him be glory forever.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Beloved brother Paul - remarkable given the tension between Peter and Paul at Antioch. Here is reconciliation and respect.
Other Scriptures (loipas graphas) - Peter places Paul's letters alongside Scripture. This is early evidence of canonical recognition.
Grow in grace and knowledge - the double object emphasizes both divine empowerment (grace) and intellectual grasp (knowledge).

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes the canonical significance: Peter treats Paul's letters as Scripture. The NT is recognizing itself. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

davids.source: Peter Davids, NICNT/Pillar 2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids notes that the doxology is Christological. Glory belongs to Jesus Christ now and forever. The letter ends as it began - focused on Christ. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Peter exhorts believers to be diligent, to be found at peace, without spot or blemish. They should count divine patience as salvation, as beloved Paul also wrote with wisdom given him. Paul's letters contain hard things that the ignorant and unstable twist, as they do other Scriptures, to their destruction. Therefore, knowing this beforehand, guard yourselves lest you be carried away by the error of lawless people and lose stability. But grow in grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. To him be glory both now and to the day of eternity. Amen.

lit.note: Chapter 3 addresses eschatological skepticism, explains divine patience, and promises cosmic renewal.

themes.note: Sovereignty dominates: God controls time and will fulfill his promise. Judgment appears in fire reserved for the ungodly. Mercy in divine patience.

discourse.title: 2 Peter 3 — The Day of the Lord and the Believer's Diligent Holiness
discourse.thesis: Scoffers will mock Christ's coming; but the Lord is not slow concerning his promise — he is patient, willing that all reach repentance. The day of the Lord will come like a thief; therefore live diligent and holy lives.

nodes.label: Reminder of prophetic and apostolic teaching (3:1-2)
nodes.text: Peter writes a second letter to stir up sincere minds by reminder, that they should remember the predictions of holy prophets and the commandment of the Lord and Savior through the apostles.

nodes.label: Scoffers and the day of the LORD (3:3-10)
nodes.text: In the last days scoffers will come, following their own evil desires, asking 'where is this coming he promised?' They deliberately overlook that long ago by God's word the heavens existed, the earth was formed out of water, and through these the world of that time was deluged. By the same word the present heavens and earth are reserved for fire on the day of judgment. With the Lord one day is as a thousand years, a thousand years as one day. The Lord is not slow concerning his promise but is patient, not wishing any to perish but all to come to repentance. But the day of the Lord will come like a thief; the heavens will pass away with a roar, the elements dissolved with fire, the earth and the works on it disclosed.

nodes.label: Live diligent and holy lives (3:11-18)
nodes.text: Since all these things will be dissolved, what kind of people ought you to be in lives of holiness and godliness, waiting and hastening the coming of the day of God! According to his promise we are waiting for new heavens and new earth in which righteousness dwells. Therefore, beloved, while you wait for these, be diligent to be found by him in peace, without spot or blemish. Count the patience of our Lord as salvation — even as Paul wrote with the wisdom given him in all his letters (some things hard to understand, which the ignorant and unstable twist, as they do other Scriptures, to their own destruction). Beware lest, carried away with the error of lawless people, you lose your stability. But grow in grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. To him be glory both now and to the day of eternity. Amen.

discourse.note: v.16's reference to Paul's letters as Scripture ('the other Scriptures') is the NT's earliest explicit recognition of Pauline letters as Scripture-equivalent — showing Pauline letters had achieved canonical-recognition by the time of 2 Peter's writing.

hebtext.note: v.3's term for last-days mockers. The verb empaizō means 'mock, ridicule.' The eschatological context of mocking-as-end-times-marker echoes 1 Tim 4:1; 2 Tim 3:1; Jude 18. Peter identifies eschatological-skepticism as itself a sign of the eschatological situation.

v.4, 12 use parousia for Christ's eschatological coming. The technical NT term covers both arrival-event and abiding-presence. Hellenistic culture used parousia for emperor-visits to provincial cities; NT theology adapts the term to describe Christ's royal-arrival in eschatological glory.

v.13's eschatological promise echoes Isa 65:17, 66:22. The 'new' (kainos) is qualitative-renewal, not chronological-replacement. The promise underwrites NT eschatology of cosmic-restoration (Rom 8:19-22; Rev 21:1) and ties to Isaiah's eschatological hope.

ppl.name: The scoffers (last-days mockers)
ppl.text: v.3-4's targeted figures who mock the second-coming promise. Their argument ('all things continue as from the beginning of creation') treats the world's apparent-stability as evidence against eschatological-rupture. Peter's response: the same word that created and once-flooded the world will judge it again. The scoffers' uniformitarian framework is itself the theological problem.

ppl.name: The 'beloved' (faithful believers, addressed throughout ch 3)
ppl.text: Peter's repeated 'beloved' (vv.1, 8, 14, 17) addresses the faithful core distinct from the false-teachers + scoffers. His pastoral burden: equip the beloved to remain stable amid scoffer-pressure and to live diligently in eschatological-hope.

ppl.name: Paul (3:15-16)
ppl.text: Peter's reference to 'our beloved brother Paul' confirms apostolic-collegiality between the two and recognises Paul's letters as authoritative-Scripture-level texts. Peter's note that Paul's letters contain 'some things hard to understand' acknowledges interpretive challenges while affirming Paul's apostolic-wisdom. The verse is foundational for understanding NT-canonical-formation: Paul's letters were already received as Scripture-equivalent by the time 2 Peter was written.

rec.text: Augustine cites 2 Pet 3:8's 'one day as a thousand years' framework in his discussions of millennial-interpretation. The verse moderates literal-millennial readings by establishing divine-time as qualitatively-different from human-time. Augustine's appropriation of the verse contributed to the development of amillennial theology.

Calvin's exposition of 'not wishing any to perish but all to come to repentance' (v.9) is contested in Reformed-Arminian debate. Calvin reads the 'all' as 'all elect' (preserving definite-atonement); Arminian readings treat it as 'all humans' (indicating universal salvific intent that humans may resist). The verse remains a key Reformed-Arminian crux.

Bauckham's analysis of ch 3 emphasises the chapter's response to specific-scoffer-arguments (uniformitarian-naturalist framework against eschatological-intervention). The chapter's apologetic-eschatological structure addresses concrete first-century challenges that recur in modern naturalist-skepticism. The chapter's argument-form remains relevant to contemporary apologetics.

rec.who: Christopher Wright, How to Read the Bible (2017)
rec.text: Wright treats 2 Pet 3:8-9 alongside Hab 2:3-4 as paired biblical responses to apparent-divine-delay: Yahweh's timing operates on different scale than human; the apparent-slowness is patient-mercy, not weakness. The texts shape Christian theology of waiting-faithfully amid unfulfilled-eschatological-hope.

thread.text: Peter's argument grounds in Genesis creation (water-formed earth) and flood (water-deluge) — establishing the pattern that the same divine-word creates, judges, and re-creates. The Genesis-typology grounds the eschatological-fire-judgment as the next iteration of established divine pattern.

'One day with the Lord is as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day' echoes Ps 90:4 ('a thousand years in your sight are like a day that has passed'). Peter applies Moses's wisdom-meditation on God's eternity to the eschatological-timing question. The verse has shaped Christian-theological thinking about divine-time.

'The day of the Lord will come like a thief' is one of the NT's most-repeated eschatological-phrases (also 1 Thess 5:2; Matt 24:43-44; Luke 12:39-40; Rev 3:3; 16:15). The shared formula across multiple NT-writers establishes thief-in-the-night as standard-imagery for the parousia's chronological-unpredictability.

Peter's 'new heavens and new earth' formula fulfils Isaiah's eschatological promise and parallels Revelation's vision. The three texts together establish the OT-NT continuity of cosmic-restoration eschatology. Christian eschatology of new-creation rests on this Isaiah-Peter-Revelation triad.

Peter's recognition of Paul's letters as Scripture establishes early-Christian-canonical recognition of the Pauline corpus. The reference assumes the recipients have access to Pauline letters and recognise them as authoritative. The verse is foundational for understanding NT canonical-formation history.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVAbove all, you must understand that in the last days scoffers will come, scoffing and following their own evil desires.KJVKnowing this first, that there shall come in the last days scoffers, walking after their own lusts,NIVThey will say, “Where is this ‘coming’ he promised? Ever since our ancestors died, everything goes on as it has since the beginning of creation.”KJVAnd saying, Where is the promise of his coming? for since the fathers fell asleep, all things continue as they were from the beginning of the creation.NIVBut do not forget this one thing, dear friends: With the Lord a day is like a thousand years, and a thousand years are like a day.KJVBut, beloved, be not ignorant of this one thing, that one day is with the Lord as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day.NIVThe Lord is not slow in keeping his promise, as some understand slowness. Instead he is patient with you, not wanting anyone to perish, but everyone to come to repentance.KJVThe Lord is not slack concerning his promise, as some men count slackness; but is longsuffering to us-ward, not willing that any should perish, but that all should come to repentance.NIVBut the day of the Lord will come like a thief. The heavens will disappear with a roar; the elements will be destroyed by fire, and the earth and everything done in it will be laid bare.KJVBut the day of the Lord will come as a thief in the night; in the which the heavens shall pass away with a great noise, and the elements shall melt with fervent heat, the earth also and the works that are therein shall be burned up.NIVBut in keeping with his promise we are looking forward to a new heaven and a new earth, where righteousness dwells.KJVNevertheless we, according to his promise, look for new heavens and a new earth, wherein dwelleth righteousness.NIVBut grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. To him be glory both now and forever! Amen.KJVBut grow in grace, and in the knowledge of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ. To him be glory both now and for ever. Amen.

coaching.tip: 'Scoffers will come in the last days... saying, Where is the promise of his coming?' (vv. 3-4). The delay of Christ's return becomes evidence against it. Peter counters: 'With the Lord one day is as a thousand years' (v. 8). Divine patience explains the delay — not slowness but mercy, waiting for repentance. The clock runs differently in heaven.

'There are some things in them [Paul's letters] that are hard to understand' (v. 16). Peter acknowledges Paul's difficulty while affirming his authority. The 'ignorant and unstable' twist Paul's letters — and other Scriptures. Note: Paul's letters are classed with Scripture. Canon recognition happens organically, even before formal declaration.

prayer_prompt: As Peter explains that the Lord is not slow in keeping His promise but is patient, not wanting anyone to perish, consider asking God whether His patience toward you should reshape how patient you are with others.
